_meta.title: Gpu Architecture Simt Warps Memory Hierarchy

questions.question: In NVIDIA's SIMT execution model, a warp consists of how many threads?
questions.explanation: A warp is always 32 threads on NVIDIA GPUs. All 32 threads execute the same instruction simultaneously. When threads diverge, both paths execute serially with masking.

questions.question: When threads in the same warp take different branches of an if-statement, what does the hardware do?
options: It throws a runtime exception for illegal divergence
It executes both paths serially, masking inactive threads for each path
It pauses the slower threads until the faster ones finish
It dynamically splits the warp into two smaller warps
questions.explanation: Warp divergence is handled by executing both paths serially. On the first path, threads that took the other branch are masked off. On the second path, the roles reverse. This halves throughput for that code section.

questions.question: Loading data from global memory (HBM) into shared memory before computation is called:
questions.explanation: Tiling loads a block of data into fast shared memory so it can be read many times during computation without re-fetching from slow global memory. In matrix multiply, this reduces global memory traffic by a factor of the tile size.

questions.question: A warp of 32 threads accesses float array A such that thread i reads A[i * 8]. This access pattern is:
options: Strided — many transactions due to non-consecutive addresses
Illegal — the hardware rejects non-unit stride
questions.explanation: Thread i accesses A[i*8], which means the 32 threads access addresses 0, 8, 16, ..., 248 — a stride of 8 floats (32 bytes). This is strided access and cannot be coalesced into a single transaction. Consecutive threads should access consecutive addresses for coalescing.

questions.question: What is SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) in GPU architecture?
options: Each thread runs a different instruction independently
A group of threads (warp/wavefront) executes the same instruction on different data each cycle
Instructions are executed sequentially across all threads
Only one thread runs at a time but switches very fast
questions.explanation: SIMT means a warp (32 threads on NVIDIA) executes the same instruction simultaneously, each on its own data. When threads diverge on a branch, both paths are executed serially (predication). This massive parallelism with simple control flow is why GPUs excel at throughput workloads.
